2026-04-17T10:50:10.7245370Z

2026-04-17T10:50:10.7255133Z ◆ Found it! In `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts` at line 284, when there are multiple errors from retries (more than 1), it throws an `AggregateError` instead of the last error (which would be a `MessagingError`). When there's only 1 error, it throws the single error directly.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7256122Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7257767Z   The test expects that the connection error is thrown as a `MessagingError`. When connecting to a non-existent namespace, the retry logic fails multiple times (multiple retry attempts), so `errors.length > 1`, which causes `AggregateError` to be thrown instead of the individual `MessagingError`.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7258871Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7259024Z

2026-04-17T10:50:10.7261048Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7261777Z ◆ Now I understand the issue. In `retry.ts`, when the retry loop fails and there are multiple errors (more than 1), it throws an `AggregateError`. When there's only 1 error, it throws that error directly. 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7262529Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7263396Z   The test expects a `MessagingError` to be thrown when connecting to a non-existent namespace. The retry mechanism accumulates all errors and if more than 1 retry is attempted, throws an `AggregateError` wrapping the individual `MessagingError`s.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7264313Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7264658Z   But when did this change happen? The git log is limited (grafted), so I can't see the full history. 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7265091Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7268252Z   I'm thinking the behavior might have shifted at some point—when `retryable` is true and the host isn't found (ENOTFOUND), it retries multiple times and then throws `AggregateError` instead of just the last error. Looking at the test failure, it's expecting a `MessagingError` but getting an `AggregateError` wrapping multiple errors instead. The core issue is whether throwing `AggregateError` on retry exhaustion is the intended behavior or a regression—the test expects a `MessagingError` to be thrown, suggesting the public API contract should remain consistent even when retries fail. This looks like a deliberate code change that may have inadvertently broken the expected error handling contract.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7270671Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7271186Z ✓ $ grep -n "AggregateError\|errors\[" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/CHANGELOG.md | head -20
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7271680Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7272015Z ✓ $ head -60 /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/CHANGELOG.md
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7272375Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7273467Z ◆ So in `core-amqp` version `4.3.5`, the change was made to throw `AggregateError` when collecting multiple errors across retries. This was released in February 2025. The EventHub test was written expecting a `MessagingError` to be thrown, but now `AggregateError` is thrown after multiple retries.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7274542Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7274906Z   The test was written before this change and is now stale. The test at line 20-26 in `client.spec.ts`:
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7275344Z   ```typescript
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7275687Z   async function validateConnectionError(promise: Promise<unknown>): Promise<void> {
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7276608Z     await expect(promise).to.be.rejected.then((err) => {
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7276949Z       expect(err)
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7277166Z         .to.be.an.instanceOf(MessagingError)
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7277591Z         .and.has.property("code", isNodeLike ? "ENOTFOUND" : "ServiceCommunicationError");
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7277999Z       return err;
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7278181Z     });
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7278448Z   }
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7278605Z   ```
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7278758Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7279795Z   This needs to be updated to handle both cases - when only 1 error occurs (returns `MessagingError`) and when multiple errors occur (returns `AggregateError`). However, for the user-facing API contract, throwing an `AggregateError` instead of a `MessagingError` is indeed a breaking change for users.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7280898Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7281559Z   The validation function needs to check whether the error is an `AggregateError` containing multiple `MessagingError` instances, or just a single `MessagingError`, and validate accordingly.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7282291Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7284135Z   Now I'm looking at the CI annotation more carefully - it shows 4 errors, which suggests all retries were exhausted. The `core-amqp 4.3.5` release notes list this as a bug fix rather than a breaking change, so the `AggregateError` behavior is intentional. But since event-hubs exposes a public API, users expect consistent error types like `MessagingError`. I need to decide whether to update the test to accept both error types, or have the event-hubs package wrap the `AggregateError` back into a `MessagingError` to maintain backward compatibility.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7286031Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7287089Z   Given that this was marked as a bug fix in core-amqp, updating the test seems like the right call. 2026-04-17T10:50:10.7316640Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7317377Z   Now let me understand the root cause better. The `AggregateError` throwing was introduced in `core-amqp 4.3.5` (February 2025). The event-hubs tests were written to expect `MessagingError` from those operations. 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7318175Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7319359Z   Since the in-repo `core-amqp` now throws `AggregateError` when there are multiple retry errors, the tests fail. I need to decide between two approaches: either update the tests to handle `AggregateError` alongside `MessagingError`, or have event-hubs unwrap the `AggregateError` and re-throw a `MessagingError` to maintain its public API contract.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7320624Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7322470Z   Looking at the semantics, the `EventHubConsumerClient` should expose a consistent error type to users, and throwing `AggregateError` would be a breaking change. The test is correctly documenting the expected behavior, so the fix belongs in the event-hubs package to catch and unwrap the `AggregateError` before re-throwing. The CI failure shows the test is expecting a `MessagingError` instance but getting an `AggregateError` instead, which confirms the validation function needs the underlying error type.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7324299Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7326044Z   The core issue is that `core-amqp` intentionally introduced `AggregateError` to surface multiple retry failures, but from a user perspective, this breaks the existing API contract—they expect a single `MessagingError` with a code like `ENOTFOUND`, not an aggregate of errors. I should look at whether there's a way to extract the most relevant error from the aggregate and return that instead. fix depends on what the desired behavior is:
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7328018Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7328500Z   Option A: Update tests to expect `AggregateError` with `MessagingError` entries (accept the breaking change, document new behavior)
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7329048Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7329385Z   Option B: Make event-hubs unwrap `AggregateError` into the last error or most relevant error
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7329802Z

2026-04-17T10:50:10.7374863Z ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/eventhub/event-hubs # Run the specific failing test file pnpm run test 2>&1 | grep -A5 "non existent namespace\|FAIL\|PASS\|AggregateError\|MessagingError\|validateConnectionError" | head -60
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7375862Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7375995Z ✓ read_bash(delay: 120, shellId: 33)
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7376402Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7376559Z ✓ read_bash(delay: 60, shellId: 33)
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7376731Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7377275Z ◆ The tests exited with code 0, meaning they passed! Let me read the full output to see if the failing tests were actually executed and whether they passed.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7377821Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7378910Z ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/eventhub/event-hubs # Run the specific failing test pnpm exec vitest run test/internal/client.spec.ts --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | grep -E "FAIL|PASS|✓|✗|×|non existent|ServiceCommunicationError|AggregateError|Error" | head -40
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7379913Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7380162Z ◆ Interesting! The tests I was expecting to fail actually PASSED locally:
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7380886Z   - `EventHubConsumerClient with non existent namespace > should throw ServiceCommunicationError for getEventHubProperties` ✓
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7381790Z   - `EventHubConsumerClient with non existent namespace > should throw ServiceCommunicationError for getPartitionProperties` ✓
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7382338Z   - etc.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7382500Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7382686Z   But there are different failures locally:
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7383784Z   - `EventHubConsumerClient with non existent event hub > should throw MessagingEntityNotFoundError for getEventHubProperties` ✗ - `expected Error: unable to verify the first certifi… { code: '…' } to be an instance of MessagingError`
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7384691Z     
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7386070Z   The local error is a TLS certificate verification issue, which makes sense for a test environment. But wait—the CI failures are about `non existent namespace` while my local failures are about `non existent event hub`. These are actually different test scenarios, so I need to look more carefully at what the CI annotations are actually reporting.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7387577Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7390061Z   Looking at the CI test output, all the failures are showing `AggregateError` being thrown instead of `MessagingError` across multiple test cases—getEventHubProperties, getPartitionProperties, getPartitionIds, and subscribe. The pattern suggests the connection to a non-existent namespace is wrapping errors in an AggregateError when the tests expect a MessagingError. sb://a` (non-existent namespace hostname "a") gives ENOTFOUND after retries, resulting in an `AggregateError`. But locally in this environment, the DNS lookup to "a" must succeed somehow (or fail differently) - maybe "a" is resolvable locally.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7392250Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7393191Z   But locally those tests PASS with the `non existent namespace` pattern. The local failures are different - `Error: unable to verify the first certifi...` for `non existent event hub` tests which connect to a real `Endpoint=sb://Host` with a valid (but non-existing) hub name.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7394196Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7394868Z   So the CI failure and local failure are about different things. The CI is on Ubuntu (ubuntu_22x_node) and there, `sb://a` DNS lookup fails and results in ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 9890 chars]
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7395546Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7396743Z ◆ The EventHub tests pass locally but with a different error for the `non existent event hub` group. The CI failure on Ubuntu involves `AggregateError` for `non existent namespace`. Let me verify the root cause more deeply.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7397530Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7398177Z ✓ $ grep -n "checkNetworkConnection\|ConnectionLostError\|retryable" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts | head -20
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7398796Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7399072Z ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure..., view_range: [160, 215])
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7399380Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7400087Z ✓ $ grep -n "defaultMaxRetries\|defaultDelayBetween" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/util/constants.ts | head -10
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7400678Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7401159Z ◆ Confirmed: default maxRetries = 3, so totalNumberOfAttempts = 4. This explains the `AggregateError { errors: [ …(4) ] }` (4 errors).
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7401703Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7401897Z   Now let me understand the flow more clearly:
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7402248Z   1. The test connects to `Endpoint=sb://a;...` (hostname = "a")
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7402630Z   2. DNS lookup for "a" fails with ENOTFOUND on Ubuntu CI
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7403073Z   3. The error is translated to `MessagingError` with code `ServiceCommunicationError`
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7403778Z   4. In `retry.ts:234-243`, since the error is `ServiceCommunicationError` and not retryable, it calls `checkNetworkConnection("a")`
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7404313Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7404463Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7405194Z   5. On Ubuntu CI, `checkNetworkConnection("a")` returns false, so the error gets reclassified as `ConnectionLostError` and becomes retryable, triggering all 4 retry attempts before throwing the aggregate error
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7406527Z   6. Locally, the network check probably returns true or "a" resolves differently, preventing the retry logic from kicking in
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7407337Z   7. The test failure is because `validateConnectionError` expects `MessagingError` but receives `AggregateError` instead
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7407851Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7408602Z   Now I'm considering whether the fix belongs in the event-hubs package (catching and unwrapping the aggregate error) or if the test itself needs to account for this retry behavior being intentional in core-amqp.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7409405Z   
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7410205Z   But really, exposing `AggregateError` to end users through `getEventHubProperties()` seems like poor API design—event-hubs should be wrapping this in a clean, single error for consumers.
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7410887Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7411481Z ✓ $ grep -rn "retry\|AggregateError" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/src/ | grep -v "node_modules\|\.js:" | head -30
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7412063Z 
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7412139Z Statistics:
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7412303Z   Turns: 48
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7412474Z   Tools: 51/51 succeeded
2026-04-17T10:50:10.7412717Z   Tokens: 2,651,634 total (2,624,521 in / 27,113 out)
